18: hosa ean desete epi tes ges estai dedomena en ourano — whatever you bind on earth will have been bound in heaven — the exact same binding/loosing language as Matthew 16:19 (the authority given to Peter) is now given to the community as a whole in the context of church discipline. Deo/luo (bind/loose) were standard rabbinic terms for declaring something forbidden or permitted (halakic ruling); here applied to the community's disciplinary decisions and intercessions. The perfects (dedemenon/lelymeron) indicate that the heavenly ratification precedes and grounds the earthly decision.
1: en ekeinē tē hōra — in that hour — The temporal marker links directly to the immediately preceding temple-tax exchange; the disciples’ question about greatness is provoked by observing Jesus’ deference.
2: proskalesámenos paidíon — having called a child — Paidíon is the diminutive of paîs, emphasizing smallness. The aorist participle proskalesámenos (having summoned) shows the child is a deliberate visual aid, not accidental.
3: straphête kai génēsthe hōs ta paidía — unless you turn and become as children — Straphête (aorist passive of stréphō) carries the sense of radical reorientation, cognate to epistréphō (repentance-turn). The aorist génēsthe demands an actual becoming, not merely childlike demeanor. The double condition (ean mē + subjunctive + subjunctive) makes entry to the kingdom contingent on both the turn and the becoming.
4: hóstis oun tapeinōsei heautón — whoever therefore humbles himself — Tapeinoō (to make low) is used here reflexively; the one who actively places himself in a position of dependence and low status. The child is not being praised for virtue but for social rank — lowest in the ancient household.
5: hòs eàn déxētai paidíon toioûto — whoever receives one such child — Déxētai (aorist subjunctive of déchomai) = receives hospitably, welcomes. The phrase epì tô̧ onómati mou (on the basis of my name) grounds hospitality to the least in identification with Christ himself.
6: hóristhos mýlos onikos — a donkey millstone — Mýlos onikos = the large upper millstone turned by a donkey (vs. the hand-mill). The image intensifies drowning: not a small stone tied around the neck but a massive grinding stone. Skandalísē (aorist subjunctive of skandalízō) = causes to stumble, causes to fall away from faith.
7: ouaì tô̧ kósmō̧ apò tôn skandálōn — woe to the world because of stumbling blocks — Ouaí is a lament-curse interjection. Skándalon (from skandalḗthron, the trigger-stick of a trap) = anything that causes one to trip and fall. The Greek distinguishes structural necessity (anánkē gàr eltheîn = for it is necessary for them to come) from personal guilt (ouaì dè tô̧ anthrṓpō̧).
8: ei dè hē cheír sou è ho poús sou skandalízei se — if your hand or foot causes you to stumble — The conditional is third-class (remote possibility; eán + present subjunctive), not endorsing literal self-mutilation but using hyperbole to insist that moral excision is preferable to total destruction. Kullón (maimed) and chōlón (lame) describe the diminished but living state that is better than hell.
9: mónophthalmon eiseltheîn eis tḕn zōḗn — one-eyed to enter into life — Monóphthalmos (one-eyed) is a compound hapax in Matthew; parallels Matthew 18:8. The repetition of the kólasin aiṓnion / pŷr aiṓnion / géennan toû pyrós vocabulary across Matthew 18:8-9 reinforces the eschatological stakes behind the community discourse.
10: hoi ángeloi autôn en ouranoîs — their angels in heaven — The phrase horáō dià pantòs tò prósōpon (they always see the face) echoes the idiom of throne-room access in Persian court protocol, here applied to the highest angels as personal guardians of the little ones. Dià pantós = continuously, not intermittently.
11: [Matthew 18:11 absent in the best manuscripts — NA28 brackets; textual insertion from Luke 19:10]
12: tí hymîn dokeî — what do you think? — A Matthean rhetorical opener inviting deliberation. Apoplanaō (has gone astray) is aorist passive suggesting the sheep was led astray, not merely wandered; emphasizing vulnerability. Tà enenḗkonta ennéa = ninety-nine, left epì tà órē (on the mountains), exposed.
13: chairei ep' autô̧ mâllon — rejoices over it more — Mâllon (more, rather) marks a comparative that overturns natural expectation: the recovered one occasions greater joy than the stable ninety-nine. The present indicative chaírei expresses the ongoing character of this rejoicing.
14: oútōs ouk éstin thélēma émprosthen toû patròs hymôn — thus it is not the will before your Father — Émprosthen = before, in the presence of. The expression hína apólētai (that one of these little ones should perish) uses the aorist subjunctive in a negated purpose clause, the strongest Greek construction for divine intention: God categorically wills none lost.
15: hýpage kai élegxon autòn metaxù soû kaì autoû mónon — go and rebuke him between you and him alone — Elégchō = expose, rebuke, convict — a legal term (cf. LXX Job 13:3). The adverb mónon (alone) and metaxý (between) insist on private confrontation first; the goal is expressed as ekérdēsas (you have gained), treating the brother as an asset to be recovered, not an enemy to be punished.
16: epì stómatos dýo martyrōn — on the mouth of two witnesses — Alludes directly to Deuteronomy 19:15 (epì stómatos dýo martyrōn kaì trîon martyrōn). The community procedure adapts Mosaic juridical law for intra-church discipline; the phrase pantò rhêma stathȩ̂ (every matter may be established) carries forensic weight.
17: éstō soi hōsper ho ethnikòs kaì ho telṓnēs — let him be to you as the Gentile and the tax collector — Ironic: Jesus elsewhere models compassion toward exactly these groups. The instruction is not about contempt but about treating the unrepentant member as outside covenant community, requiring re-evangelization rather than fellowship discipline. Parakúō (ignores, refuses to listen) governs the escalation.
19: symphōnḗsōsin ek tês gês perì pantòs prágmatos — agree on earth concerning any matter — Symphōnéō (agree together, be in harmony) is the verb behind English 'symphony.' The condition hó eàn aítēsōntai (whatever they ask) is governed by the communal discernment context, not a blank promise for any private desire.
20: hou gár eisin dýo è treîs synagménoi eis tò emòn ónoma — where two or three are gathered into my name — Synágō eis ónoma (gather into/around a name) is the assembly formula for Jewish court decisions (Pirkei Avot 3:2 uses the same pattern for Torah study). Jesus substitutes his own name for the divine name, an implicit Christological claim.
21: posákis hamartḗsei eis emè ho adelphós mou — how many times will my brother sin against me — Peter’s proposed limit of seven times invokes the perfect number; he likely thinks he is being generous. Aphḗsō autô̧ (shall I forgive him) uses the future indicative, framing forgiveness as a measured policy decision.
22: héōs hebdomēkontákis heptá — up to seventy-seven times — The phrase alludes to Genesis 4:24 (LXX: hebdomēkontákis heptá), Lamech’s boast of unlimited vengeance. Jesus inverts the vengeance-escalation formula of Lamech into an forgiveness-escalation: where Lamech claimed seventy-seven-fold retribution, disciples extend seventy-seven-fold pardon. Some MSS read hebdomēkontakis heptá = seventy times seven (490), but the Lamech allusion favors 77.
23: hōmoiṓthē hē basileía tôn ouranôn anthrṓpō̧ basilêi — the kingdom of heaven was likened to a king — The aorist passive hōmoiṓthē opens a parable formula. Synaîrai lógon (to settle accounts) is commercial terminology from papyrus receipts; lógon here = ledger account.
24: myríōn talántōn — ten thousand talents — Mýrioi = ten thousand, the highest numeral in Greek, hence 'myriad.' One talent = 6,000 denarii (a laborer’s ~20-year wage). Ten thousand talents = roughly 200,000 years’ wages — a deliberately impossible sum, signaling that the debt is not meant to be realistic but to represent unpayable moral indebtedness before God.
25: apodídōmi pánt' ósōn échō — sell all that he has — Prathênai (to be sold, aorist passive infinitive) with family included reflects ancient debt-slavery practice. The servant’s pledge apodídōmi pánt' (I will repay everything) is mathematically absurd given the sum, signaling desperation rather than credible promise.
26: prosekýnei autô̧ légōn — he prostrated himself before him saying — Proskyneō = full prostration (not merely bow), the posture of absolute submission before royalty. The imperfect prosekýnei suggests ongoing/prolonged prostration as he pleaded.
27: esplanchnísthē ho kýrios toû doúlou ekeínou — the lord of that servant was moved with compassion — Splagchnízomai recurs (cf. Matthew 9:36; Matthew 14:14; Matthew 15:32) — visceral compassion seated in the inner organs. Apélyen (released) is the verb for releasing a debt/prisoner; aphêken (forgave) adds the legal acquittal. Both verbs in aorist, decisive and complete acts.
28: hekatòn dēnaríōn — one hundred denarii — ~100 days’ wages, a real but repayable sum — the sharp contrast with 10,000 talents (Matthew 18:24) is structurally central to the parable’s indictment. Épnigen (was choking/strangling him) is graphic physical violence, showing the released servant’s ruthlessness.
29: pesṑn oun eis toùs pódas autoû parekálei — falling at his feet he begged — The vocabulary is identical to the first servant’s plea to the king (Matthew 18:26), making the parallel structurally precise and the irony unmistakable.
30: ouk ḗthelen dè — but he was not willing — The imperfect ḗthelen (was willing/wanting) denotes ongoing refusal; a sustained decision not a momentary lapse. Apelthṑn ébalen = went and threw him into prison, the aorist pairing showing decisive action.
31: elypḗthēsan sphodra — they were greatly grieved — Lypaō = to grieve, pain deeply; sphodra = greatly, intensely. The other servants’ moral anguish moves them to report (diesará¸£hṓthēsan = explained fully, spread out the whole account) to the master.
32: doûle ponēré — wicked servant — The address ponēré (wicked, evil) echoes the characterization of the evil one throughout Matthew. Pâsan tḕn opheliéan ekeínēn (all that debt) recaps the total; the king’s mercy was not partial cancellation but total remission.
33: ouk édei kaì sè eleêsai — was it not necessary for you to have mercy — Édei (it was necessary, imperfect of deî) frames mercy toward the co-servant as a moral obligation arising from the mercy received, not merely an option. The aorist infinitive eleêsai refers to a specific act of mercy that should have occurred.
34: orgistheìs ho kýrios autoû paredōken autòn — having become angry the master handed him over — Orgízomai (to become angry) aorist participle; the anger results in judicial action. Paredōken (handed over) to the torturers (basanistâs) until full payment — echoing the eschatological language of judgment and eternal consequence.
35: outōs kaì ho patḗr mou ho ouránios poiḗsei hymîn — so also my heavenly Father will do to you — The parable’s application is direct and future: poiḗsei (will do) = divine judgment. Apò tôn kardiôn hymôn (from your hearts) specifies that surface forgiveness is insufficient; the forgiveness must be genuine and inward.
